behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: failure can occur at the handoff between file discovery/parsing and execution scheduling (e.g., parser extracts numeric prefix correctly but executor receives unsorted list). Integration -> System rejected: behavior text is only about ordering by filename prefix; no required real OS/network/subprocess behavior is specified, so in-process filesystem and DB fakes can expose the ordering bug. Higher levels rejected: no AI/ML output dependency and no multi-agent sequence in this behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner with a temp migrations set (e.g., 001, 010, 002) and a fake/in-process DB adapter that records executed migration names; assert execution order is 001, 002, 010 and metadata inserts follow the same order.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: the failure boundary is the interface between runner logic and migration-state storage (_migrations lookup/update across runs). Integration -> System rejected: behavior text is about skip-on-second-run semantics, which can be reproduced with in-process module wiring and persisted fake state; it does not require real OS/network timing behavior. Higher levels rejected: no model-output correctness and no agentic chain.
behaviors.test_description: Execute runner twice against the same in-process test repository/DB adapter state. First run applies migration and records it; second run should execute zero SQL for that migration and leave metadata unchanged except expected timestamps/counters.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: failure involves handoff across runner, transaction wrapper, and migration executor (error propagation must trigger rollback and halt). Integration -> System proof: rollback correctness depends on real PostgreSQL transactional behavior when a statement fails; in-process fakes can mask partial-commit/transaction-state bugs. Agentic rejected: behavior text is SQL failure/rollback control flow, not model output quality. Workflow rejected: no multiple agentic steps.
behaviors.test_description: Against a real Postgres instance, run migrations where one migration does a write then triggers SQL error; assert written rows are absent after run (rolled back), failed migration is not marked applied, and later migration files are not executed.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: bug appears at module boundary where migration selection/claiming interacts with shared metadata state. Integration -> System proof: behavior explicitly requires two instances starting at the same time (separate processes) against the same DB; real process scheduling/DB locking-race behavior is required and mocks can hide race conditions. Agentic rejected: correctness does not depend on model output. Workflow rejected: not a multi-agentic pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two runner processes concurrently against the same real Postgres DB and same migration directory; after both exit, verify each migration’s effects appear exactly once and _migrations contains one record per migration with no duplicates.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: ordering bug occurs at bootstrap handoff between metadata-initialization component and migration executor. Integration -> System rejected: behavior text is sequencing ('created before any migration file is applied'), which can be validated with in-process wiring that captures issued operations in order; no mandatory real OS/network/subprocess failure mode is stated. Higher levels rejected: no AI/ML dependency and no multi-agentic sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Run against a fresh in-process test adapter that logs DDL/DML calls; assert first relevant operation is create _migrations table, then migration file SQL execution begins, and metadata writes succeed.
